sentences.text: The journey from-- from-- to Vladivostok?
[INAUDIBLE]

Well, the-- the journey as such, apart from the three days in Moscow and the experiences there, which may be of interest in terms of the observing life in Russia prior to the war, and seeing certain moments of people standing in a queue waiting for bread before the war started.
And that was in Moscow, not in the hinterland.

And so-- but-- but what I would maybe try to put to you is this-- the trip to Moscow-- from-- the trip from Vilna to Moscow, and then when we finally set off on the long journey of up via the Urals right all the way to Vladivostok, it was maybe-- it was not eventful, in terms of-- because you were in a small space, in a coupe with four people.
We had to sleep in that coupe and so on.
But in terms of emotions, it was very eventful [INAUDIBLE].
I can only judge myself by my own emotions, because you had a-- a composite of-- of realizations, and awarenesses.
On the one hand, you were aware that you are escaping Europe.
You are Leaving Europe behind.
And especially, you are getting-- you are just about to finalize the last act of outwitting Stalin.
We have outwitted Hitler already.
I have, personally.
And I am in the last act of outwitting Stalin.
So we-- that sort of inner realization of an awareness that, somehow, miraculous as that may sound-- and it was, and it was.
To get a transit visa from-- and to get all this, too, and to be actually physically on that train, and so on.

And, yet, you didn't trust yourself even to think in terms of freedom yet, because as long as you knew that you are under-- in-- in a Russian train in Russian territory, and you are being watched- and you are being watched undoubtedly-- then it had to be only between your mind and your soul, so to speak, that those realizations could be-- could have a sort of a dialogue, not even with next door person.
You suspected or assume that other person is sharing the same sort of emotion, the same sort of realizations as yourself.
But that is an assumption.

And we did not dare-- when we arrived, finally, in Vladivostok, and we have transferred from the train on to that boat, we had a Russian military officer from the Coast Guard accompanying us on the boat right through the territorial waters of Russia.
That's right, of course, of course.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And it was not so simple.

I had an experience in-- at the time of going through my luggage with papers, where I had a copy of my birth certificate saying that I am from Bialystock, whilst, in fact, I gave my original story as being a shoemaker's son from Warsaw, because I didn't want to give too much of my identity.
And the man who looked at my papers, [INAUDIBLE] paper.
And when I saw him opening this piece of paper, it shows an extract, post-war, had it with the hammer and sickle.
I didn't have the original.
We had to ask for one.
And we got an extract from the new government [INAUDIBLE] the Russian government [INAUDIBLE] Bialystock.
If he would have opened that piece of paper one more opening, I would have been finished.
I would have been sent away in Siberia.
It was folded in four.
He opened it in two, and closed it again.
He never saw the text.
That was-- that was on the-- I'm not sure if that was in Vilna when we went through customs, or in Moscow that we went through customs.
It was one of the-- possibly in Vilna, because we were entering Soviet space.
And it was absolutely a miracle.
When I saw that men pick up this green grey piece of paper, which I recognized on the other side by the color.
I thought, that's it.
That's the end of me.
And he opened it in two, and folded it back again, and put it in the papers.
So, as I say, the-- by the time when we came to Vladivostok, and we transferred to that cattle boat, and we had that officer with us, we still-- no one dared breathe.

When we saw this man going down on the rope ladder from our boat into his accompanying boat to take him back home, when we saw him off that boat and we were on the open seas, everybody expressed a sigh of relief which was, obviously, mixed with a feeling of uncertainty, because we went-- we went into the unknown.
Today, when you say Japan and Japanese, it's-- it is part of every day.
You drive Japanese cars.
You use Japanese products.
Japanese people are all around you.
You encounter them. 60 Years ago, the word Japan and Japanese was just a fantasy.
To come face to face with it, or to land in Japan, was just something unreal.
And, in fact, by the time that we survived the journey-- it was a terrible journey in the straits.
And, you know, straits are always the worst place to-- and we left behind the Russian railways, and we saw the Russian people in their dirty [RUSSIAN] they were called.
That is parkas there.
The locomotive drivers, and his assistants, and the firemen, and so on, everybody dirty, and just sloppy, and so on.
And we walked out in Japan.

And the first thing we saw on the railway yards, next to the shipping [INAUDIBLE], we saw a Japanese fellow in a light blue [INAUDIBLE] uniform with white gloves-- with white gloves-- with a-- with a basket on his back, and a long stick with a pointed iron nail, picking up every little piece of paper from the rails to make sure that there is no waste, or no-- nothing dirty, or nothing disorderly.
And then we looked at the locomotive standing by.
And the people on the locomotive shoving the coal into the-- into the oven wore white gloves.
And you had the feeling of contrast of cultural-- and we are coming to-- we are coming to an Asian culture.
We are leaving European culture behind.
And we are coming to Asian culture.
And that was the Asian culture.
And it was February.
It was wintertime.
And those who had colds wore masks to protect the other people, not to give them their cold.
And that was the immediate encounter and comparison, that it was visual.
And, yet, it was [INAUDIBLE] because it prompted you to think, and make comparisons about what you have just left, and what you just encountered that you never-- never imagined.
Of course, the language was a jumble of noises that you couldn't make out, and so on.

But their behavior, when we settled in there, and we had been given-- it was provided for us, because the joint provided funds, and the local committee Kobe of-- there were some Russian Jews who lived in Kobe.
They came from [JAPANESE].
And they had been doing business in Japan in those days exporting Japanese fine cloth, which in those days they weren't exporting Toyotas and Mazdas in those days.
They were exporting textiles, and other such items.
To-- so they were buying or exporting this stuff to other parts of the world.
So there was a Jewish community with a synagogue, and organization-- organization-- organization-- organization.
And they have taken up houses, old houses, and provided mattresses on the floor.
And we had-- we went into already prepared lodgings.
We were 11-- in my room, there were 11 people, because the floor took 11 mattresses.
So there were 11 people.
Next to me was lying Henry Korngold, was lying next to me on the floor.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were given one yen and 10 cents per day for food out of this communal thing.

And I think I've told you-- I've mentioned to you the encounter with the banker Szeroszewski, who was standing in front of me in that same queue waiting for that-- for that one yen a day, the man who was the most prominent Jewish banker in Poland, whom-- whom all the old maids-- Jewish maids of Poland entrusted with their dowries that they never used.
That men stood in front of me waiting for the same one yen sustenance to be able to pull through the day, because [INAUDIBLE] whatever he possessed was somewhere else.
And in the meantime, he came-- he came with nothing.
So that was-- that was-- and we were there-- we were living there for 10 months.
And there's a whole story how it was extended.
The prime minister was involved.
It was quite a story, because officially, we were supposed to be there 10 days only, no more.

And from 10 days-- and if it were not, as I said before in interview, if it were not for the Japanese decision to attack Pearl Harbor and enter the war against America, we would have stayed there longer.
But the minute they've taken that decision, they have packed us up, and have sent us through Shanghai.
And we arrived in Shanghai on-- in October.
And the 12th of December was Pearl Harbor.
And that was-- and that was it.
And we were just there for the duration.
No, no.
But I must say in their favor, and I think it ought to be underlined, because Japan normally doesn't eat bread.
They eat rice.
And when they have decided to go into war, they had to start stockpiling food.
The first thing-- for an army and for a population, you have to stockpile food.
And, yet, we were given every day ample amount of bread to-- white bread to eat, European bread.
We were not short of it.
We were not short of it.
We were-- we had once a day to a restaurant to have a warm lunch, a private-- [?
Mama San ?] used to cook it.
And we used to eat their fish every day, a warm meal, for a few-- for a few shillings, whatever it was.
It was-- we managed.
We managed to go through the day.
We were not hungry in Japan.
No, no, no.

Considering that they were members of the Axis, they were partners with Hitler, and Mussolini in the tripartite, the Axis pact, they were-- in terms of their attitude towards us in Japan itself, was perfectly in order.
It was perfectly in order.
It was by intervention of members of the Jewish community there, who intervened at the highest level.
The story is that one of them went to see the prime minister at the time to talk to him.
And the Japanese prime minister suggested to him that they go and walk in the garden.
He didn't want to have that discussion in his office, because it could have been bugged.
And he told the Jewish representative of the Kobe community-- and he said, you have never been here.
You have never spoken to me.
Go and make your arrangements with the local prefecture of police.
And they will handle it on a local basis.
As long as it doesn't come officially to the notice of the government of Japan, this matter can be handled on a less formal way and extended.
And that is how it happened.
And there was-- they've invited another Japanese person.
There was a friend there.
And it's a whole story how it all came about.
It's not for this interview.

But we have, as I said, benefited from 10 days to 10 months of reasonable accommodation, and-- and a formal [INAUDIBLE] from the Japanese government, and people which was not to be in any way denigrated or questioned.
Well, it was a very short notice.
There was very short notice.
And they didn't have to give us more than short notice.
We didn't have any businesses to liquidate.
We didn't have any houses to sell.
We didn't have much with us in the form of luggage, and baggage, and so on.
The few things that we carried with us, they could have given us an hour's notice and we would have been gone.
But they gave us a very beautiful boat with a very nice trip from Japan to-- the modern, new boat.
It was a holiday.
It was a holiday trip on the upper deck.
We were sunning ourselves in the Pacific.
And we arrived in Shanghai.
And that's how it happened.
The exact date-- there is a document.
We will find it.
I've got an exact date.
I've got a document of landing in Shanghai.
On our arrival Shanghai, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Shanghai had a-- two Jewish communities.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had a Vladivostok community of Russian Jews who lived there, excuse me, who were originally from [RUSSIAN] and came south down, because Shanghai was an open place.
And anybody could come and go.
They didn't have visas there.
They were settled at the French concession, who lived in the French Concession.

And there was a Sephardi community of Baghdadi Jews amongst-- who came there as a result of this extraordinary success of the Sassoons and the [NON-ENGLISH], who became world famous starting from the Opium Wars in the 1840s, and so on, who amassed fortunes and so on.

And then the people from-- from Baghdad started coming and claiming-- and claiming a relation status with those-- there were poor people and so on-- in such a way that there was quite a sizable Sephardi Jewish community.
But they were quite separate.

They had two separate organizations, two different synagogues there, because they are quite a different order of-- not order of [? service ?], but the-- the music of the service, the singing, and so on, it's quite different, quite a different world.
In fact, so much so that my first business in Shanghai I've done with a Sephardic Jew trading with him in Hebrew, because my English wasn't yet of the-- of a standard to do business.
And he didn't speak Russian, or Polish, or German, and so on.
So Hebrew was the common denominator.
I struck the first deal in Hebrew.
Oh, my occupations in Shanghai were varied, very varied.
I didn't know that I'm so capable.
The Shanghai discovered in me things I never knew that I had.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At first, we were-- you know it was-- Shanghai the sort of place where business is everything.
Human life isn't worth much, because the Chinese people used to die from hunger, and cold.
Used to collect every morning in the wintertime, the corpses lying in the streets, and in the gutters, and so on.
The money was the ruling order and value of that society.
Chinese are the business people [INAUDIBLE] you well know all over the world.
So there was an opportunity and a possibility in that open city for anyone with any initiative to do something.

Next to the existing Russian Jewish community, with whom we could-- we could identify in terms of language, because most of those who spoke Russian-- certainly, those who spoke only Polish also had a Slavic affinity of language.
And [INAUDIBLE] with our mentality now processes of thinking, we are the same.
So we had an immediate affinity.
We lived among them in the French concession.
And to the extent to which they could help us, or give us some merchandise to go and sell, or to bring, or-- to that extent, people started to just unwind, and do something.
Money exchange was a major business in Shanghai in those days.
The exchange shops in Shanghai were something to be seen to be believed.
Any currency, anything.
It was the days before computers, and before-- before-- they had their Chinese abacus-- excuse me.

And they used to sit with two telephones, talking to two different-- listening to two different people from-- and-- and-- and with one hand doing the calculation on the abacus, and dealing straight away with also two people.
It was a sight to see, because they are-- their agility-- their agility, both in terms of their fingers, and their mind, and their mathematical capacity, was something to be extraordinary.
And what was so strange about it is that some Jewish refugees who were smart, and had the knack for these sort of things, started trading outside their shops, next to them-- traded currency.
It was legal.
It was not Russia.
You could handle any currency.
You could buy pounds, and dollars, in whatever currency it was, buy and sell.
Everything was-- everything was a commodity.
In war time, American dollars were certainly a commodity.
That's for sure.
There was no better commodity than America dollars.
So people started buying and selling.
And then the market required different things.
One day, the market wanted razor blades.
I mean, it was war time-- war time economy.
It's not that-- there is a demand from the market that the market requires something.

So we used to go into the back lanes of the Chinatown in the-- penetrating right into the-- and we saw the authentic Chinese life, not in the band with-- but we saw that the old Chinese people in their silk, and [NON-ENGLISH] with their thing.
And some of them still carried their long [? plates, ?] and so on.
And we found-- today, we needed blades, we bought blades.
Tomorrow, they wanted needles.
We bought needles.
Nobody opened the needles.
Most probably, they would have opened them, they would have found that they are so late and long staying there in those black lanes, they were rusted most probably.
But you didn't need the commodity to use.
You only needed it to buy and sell.
That is the nature of wartime activity.
But, somehow or other, people just traded-- did-- one thing you couldn't do in China, you couldn't go to work, because you couldn't compete with the coolie.
The coolie worked for a bowl of rice.
So you couldn't compete with the coolie in terms of-- so you had to do something else, which was not labor.
And that is how-- and then, of course, that was the first year.
And then came the proclamation, when the Germans persuaded the Japanese to create a ghetto for us.
And the Butcher of Warsaw came to Shanghai to help them organize a ghetto.
Somebody will know it.
I don't remember it anymore.
I don't remember.
I knew it.
But I can't remember it now.
And a proclamation was issued.
And let me go back for a minute.
The problem in Shanghai in the first year was that people came there beginning of winter, most often single people, without clothing.
The winters are very cold, and bitter cold in Shanghai.
And the summers and unbearably hot.
And they had nothing.
Somehow, they scrambled together to get a roof over their heads.
They couldn't cook for themselves.
They couldn't-- they couldn't go and buy.
They couldn't eat the Chinese food.
So a committee of Russian women, under the auspices of the general community, of the Russian Jewish community, instituted a kitchen in Shanghai.
And the man who brought the idea, and brought it about, was a Polish Jew by the name of [?
Leatherman, ?] Mr.
Leatherman. ?]
And a number of Russian Jewish women formed themselves a committee.
And they used to go every day to the market and buy truckloads of food every day fresh from the market.
We used to put out 250 meals a day for a nominal-- one Shanghai dollars, which was just nominal.
And one day, this man approached me out of the blue.
And I hardly knew him.
I was not an acquaintance with him.
And he says to me that he wants to talk to me.
And he says to me that he has got an opportunity to be evacuated to London by the Polish ambassador.
There were a few places they have got.
And he was one of the people whom they have recognized for his activities, and so on, and wanted to take him out.
But he will only go if he has got somebody to hand over the kitchen.
And he wants me to take it over.
He wants me to take it over.
And I looked at him in astonishment.
I was 21 years old.
There was some very, very prominent people, industrialists of note, and doctors, and lawyers, and so on.
Why me?
You are nobody.
To make a long story short, I have played for time.
It didn't help me.
I had to say yes.
And I had to take over the kitchen.
And I ran it single-handedly until the proclamation of the ghetto came into being.

And when everybody had to go to [INAUDIBLE], where the ghetto was, where the German Jewish community lived already. 17,000 German Jews came there during the early '30s before the war, when they started running away from Hitler.
Then that had to be wound up.
And then the Jewish community that was-- the new Jewish hospital in the French concession invited me to run that hospital for them.
And I became the general manager of that hospital.
No, that was outside.
I was the only man who, by the rules of the proclamation-- the proclamation provided that people working in a hospital are exempted from the-- from the forced evacuation into the ghetto.
And that was just a heaven sent opportunity.
And I jumped at it, of course.
And I lived on the premises in the hospital.
I ran the hospital for two and a half years.
I visited that ghetto.
Not-- not frequently.
Not frequently, but I went there.
After it was closed, I was not going there at all, because I didn't have the documents.
I was not part of that-- at times, I felt bad about it, because people are envious of my exceptional situation.
But there was a moment-- didn't you [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
OK.
